elements.sent-2411-001: Using high-accuracy numerical relativity waveforms, we confirm the presence of numerous overtones of the $\ell=2$, $m=2$ quasinormal mode early in the ringdown of binary black hole mergers.
elements.sent-2411-002: We do this by demonstrating the stability of the mode amplitudes at different fit times, ruling out the possibility that a linear superposition of modes unphysically fits a highly nonlinear part of the waveform.
elements.sent-2411-003: We also find a number of previously unidentified subdominant second-order quasinormal modes in the $(2,2)$ mode.
elements.sent-2411-004: Even though these modes are mathematically nonlinear, they nevertheless confirm the validity of perturbation theory as a good approximation for describing much of the ringdown.
elements.sent-2411-005: When two black holes orbit each other, they radiate energy via gravitational waves and eventually merge to form a single black hole.
elements.sent-2411-006: The gravitational waveform rises to a maximum amplitude and then settles down in a ringdown phase as the remnant black hole sheds its distortions and evolves toward a final state of equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-007: Close enough to equilibrium, the final black hole can be approximated by linear perturbation theory of the Kerr metric~\cite{Teukolsky:1973ha .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In this approximation, the radiation is dominated by a set of quasinormal modes (QNMs).
elements.sent-2411-009: The QNMs are damped oscillations of the remnant black hole, with characteristic frequencies determined by the mass and the spin of the remnant.
elements.sent-2411-010: Each QNM frequency, $\omega_{\ell m n}$, is complex valued, encoding the oscillation frequency and damping time.
elements.sent-2411-011: Here $\ell$ and $m$ label the angular harmonic of a particular mode.
elements.sent-2411-012: The final index $n$ of $\omega_{\ell m n}$ is the overtone number.
elements.sent-2411-013: It labels the eigenvalues of the radial equation for the perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-014: While the $n=0$ mode, or fundamental mode, was often of primary interest in ringdown studies, the overtones, $n>0$, were often neglected and considered subdominant.
elements.sent-2411-015: Recent work~\cite{Giesler:2019uxc challenged this notion, demonstrating that overtones are important for modeling binary black hole ringdowns and that the inclusion of overtones seems to allow for accurate modeling of the $(2,2)$ component of the waveform as early as its peak.}
elements.sent-2411-016: This result turned out to be controversial and was met with claims of overfitting and some doubts about whether the overtones are in fact physical~\cite{Mourier:2020mwa,Jaramillo:2020tuu,Forteza:2021wfq,Baibhav:2023clw,Cheung:2023vki,Zhu:2023mzv,Nee:2023osy .}
elements.sent-2411-017: A more in-depth discussion of the connection between this previous work~\cite{Giesler:2019uxc and the current work can be found in Sec.~\ref{sec:prev_work .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: An important issue is how to distinguish a linear superposition of modes that is physical from a superposition that merely provides a good fit, that is, overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-019: A good criterion is that if the modes are really there, their amplitudes should remain constant as the start time of the fit is varied, apart from the exponential decay of each mode with its expected decay constant.
elements.sent-2411-020: Part of the reason for skepticism in the papers cited above is that they could not demonstrate that the amplitudes were constant, especially at early times and for higher overtones.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we investigate the presence of linear QNMs, including overtones, and second-order QNMs~\cite{London:2014cma,Mitman:2022qdl,Cheung:2022rbm,Ma:2022wpv in the $(2,2)$ component of the ringdowns of high-accuracy numerical relativity (NR) simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Our primary focus is a theoretical understanding of the ringdown, with a goal of determining the contributions of overtones and second-order QNMs to the ringdowns of binary black holes.
elements.sent-2411-024: We aim to determine the physical picture of the ringdown and how much of it is described by perturbation theory, either first or second order.
elements.sent-2411-025: Whether the modes we identify in this work can be detected in practice with a certain detector or sensitivity is a separate question that should be addressed in future work.
elements.sent-2411-026: Throughout this work, we focus on two particular NR simulations from the Simulating eXtreme Spacetimes (SXS) collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-027: Both simulations are equal-mass aligned-spin binaries and both are extremely accurate.
elements.sent-2411-028: The high accuracy of these waveforms is due to improved methods in extracting the waveforms from the numerical evolutions.
elements.sent-2411-029: In particular, we use waveforms that have been extracted using a Cauchy evolution via SpECTRE's Cauchy-characteristic evolution (CCE) module~\cite{Moxon:2020gha,Moxon:2021gbv,spectre .}
elements.sent-2411-030: This enables us to not only obtain much higher-accuracy waveforms, but because CCE also computes the Weyl scalars, we can map the NR system to the superrest frame of the remnant black hole~\cite{MaganaZertuche:2021syq,Mitman:2024uss using Bondi-van der Burg-Metzner-Sachs (BMS) frame fixing~\cite{Mitman:2021xkq,Mitman:2022kwt,Mitman:2024uss .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: This last step is important for removing mode-mixed QNMs that arise from being in the wrong BMS frame.
elements.sent-2411-032: If these spurious modes are not removed but left unaddressed, they effectively increase the noise floor and make fitting highly challenging.
elements.sent-2411-033: Additional details regarding the NR waveforms and pre-processing can be found in Sec.~\ref{sec:waveformdata .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Beyond this, an additional improvement over previous fitting techniques is the use of a much more robust nonlinear least squares algorithm called variable projection.
elements.sent-2411-035: We make use of this algorithm to perform agnostic searches for QNMs within the ringdown, identifying the best-fitting complex frequencies of unknown QNMs.
elements.sent-2411-036: For a detailed discussion of variable projection, see Sec.~\ref{sec:dampedsinusoids .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Identifying and extracting individual QNMs from the ringdowns of NR waveforms is a challenging task.
elements.sent-2411-038: To highlight some of the subtleties associated with fitting many modes in the presence of noise, we first treat a simplified analytic case and discuss the complications that arise.
elements.sent-2411-039: In particular, we show how sensitive the highly damped modes are to small amounts of simulated numerical noise.
elements.sent-2411-040: In Sec.~\ref{sec:fitting we introduce a technique for handling the most sensitive modes and show how this allows for better resolution of mode amplitudes as a function of time.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We use this same technique in Sec.~\ref{sec:results to uncover the many QNMs in the ringdowns of our two highly accurate NR waveforms.}
elements.sent-2411-042: These analyses confirm that the overtones are physical and that they can be stably extracted from binary black hole ringdowns.
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, we also show a number of previously unidentified second-order QNMs present in the dominant $(\ell=2,m=2)$ component of the ringdown.
elements.sent-2411-044: While the second-order modes are generally subdominant, they are in principle detectable, and could serve as unique probes of the nonlinear aspects of binary black hole mergers.
elements.sent-2411-045: Lastly, we explore the accuracy of using QNMs to predict remnant parameters and discuss the connection between unmodeled nonlinearities and biased estimation.
elements.sent-2411-046: A note on our conventions: we take $c=G=1$ and use the Moreschi-Boyle conventions of Refs.~\cite{Boyle2016,Mitman:2021xkq,Mitman:2022kwt,Iozzo:2021vnq,Moreschi:1988pc,Moreschi:1998mw,Dain:2000lij .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In previous work~\cite{Giesler:2019uxc , we explored the contribution of overtones to binary black hole ringdowns.}
elements.sent-2411-048: There we showed that the inclusion of overtones provides an accurate estimate of the underlying remnant mass and spin as early as the peak\footnote{In this paper, the peak always refers to the peak amplitude of the (2,2) mode of the strain.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The time of this peak can differ significantly from that of other mode amplitudes, or of energy fluxes, or of quantities like $\Psi_4$.. of the strain.
elements.sent-2411-050: Ref.~\cite{Giesler:2019uxc also showed that a model with overtones provides an excellent description for the post-peak signal, with residuals comparable to the numerical error in the waveforms used in that study.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Showing that amplitudes are constant up to the expected decay rates, a much stricter requirement for proving the presence of overtones, proved to be more difficult and Ref.~\cite{Giesler:2019uxc could only reliably extract up to $n=2$ (with rough evidence for $n=3$ and $n=4$).}
elements.sent-2411-052: In this work, we find that with improved high-accuracy waveforms we can reliably extract the numerical decay of overtones well beyond $n=2$.
elements.sent-2411-053: These improved waveforms, along with improved fitting techniques, yield residuals at the $10^{-8}$ level, whereas the prior work had numerical noise at a significantly higher level of $\sim 10^{-5 .$ This lower noise floor allows us to identify previously unidentifiable modes and improves our ability to numerically extract individual modes over a much wider range of amplitudes and time.}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, with a lowered noise floor also comes a much higher standard for the model.
elements.sent-2411-055: Under this heightened scrutiny, while the model still provides accurate predictions for the remnant parameters and excellent residuals, some of the modes can not be consistently extracted at times very close to the peak.
elements.sent-2411-056: An unmodeled, unidentifiable contribution to the waveform in the immediate post-peak region seems to spoil the stability of the modes when attempting to extract them numerically.
elements.sent-2411-057: The time one should wait after the peak of the strain will depend on the system and appears to depend on the remnant spin.
elements.sent-2411-058: Higher spin systems require a longer wait time: as can be seen in Sec.~\ref{sec:results , stable amplitudes are not achievable until ${}\sim4M$ after the peak in a moderately spinning case and ${}\sim8M$ post-peak in a high-spin case.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Although these times are post-peak with respect to the $(2,2)$ component of the strain, each time roughly corresponds to the peak luminosity, over all $(\ell,m)$, for that system.
elements.sent-2411-060: We discuss the issue of start time and the effect of early unmodeled contributions on the accuracy of our QNM model more in Sec.~\ref{sec:eps .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Linear perturbations of the Kerr metric describing a rotating black hole can be readily determined by solving a wave-type equation for a complex scalar $\Psi$ by separation of variables~\cite{Teukolsky:1972my,Teukolsky:1973ha .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The solution from doing so can be decomposed into multipoles $\Psi_{\ell m}$ by using spin-weighted spheroidal harmonics~\cite{Teukolsky:1972my,Teukolsky:1973ha,Press:1973zz : \begin{equation}\Psi_{\ell m}\sim\int d\omega\,e^{-i\omega t}\sum_{\ell m} R_{\ell m}(r,\omega). \label{eq:perts}\end{equation} Here the modes $R_{\ell m}$ are a solution of a radial equation analogous to a quantum mechanical radial equation with some effective potential.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\Psi_{\ell m}\sim\int d\omega\,e^{-i\omega t}\sum_{\ell m} R_{\ell m}(r,\omega). \label{eq:perts}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-063: At large $r$, $\Psi$ encodes the two independent polarizations of the gravitational wave $h$.
elements.sent-2411-064: The ringdown solution we seek for $R_{\ell m}$ describes radiation leaving the domain both at infinity and at the surface of the black hole---an eigenvalue problem for frequency $\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Since energy is being dissipated, the eigenfrequencies $\omega_{\ell mn}$ are complex.
elements.sent-2411-066: The index $n$ labels the radial eigenvalues, with $n=0$ corresponding to the least-damped mode, $n=1$ the next least damped, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-067: Modes for $n>0$ are called overtones, although they have very different behavior than overtones in other physical systems.
elements.sent-2411-068: At late times, the $1/r$ piece of Eq.~\eqref{eq:perts at large $r$ becomes an outgoing wave \begin{equation}\Psi_{\ell m}=\sum_{\ell mn} C_{\ell mn}e^{-i\omega_{\ell mn}(t-r_*)},\end{equation} where $r_*$ is a radial ``tortoise'' coordinate.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The gravitational wave strain $h$ measured by a detector consists of two polarization states.
elements.sent-2411-070: These are usually combined into a single complex strain that is decomposed into modes using spin-weighted spherical harmonics.
elements.sent-2411-071: We will initially ignore the distinction between spherical and spheroidal harmonics, so the strain modes at a fixed large $r_*$ are \begin{equation}\label{eq:model} h_{\ell m}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} C_{\ell mn}e^{-i\omega_{\ell mn}t}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-072: In Eq.~\eqref{eq:model we have renormalized the amplitudes $C_{\ell mn}$ in going from $\Psi$ to $h$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Each term in this sum represents a damped sinusoid and gives the mathematical description of a single QNM with indices $(\ell,m,n)$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Note that $C_{\ell m n}$ refers specifically to the complex-valued amplitudes associated with each mode and that we use $A_{\ell m n \equiv |C_{\ell m n |$ to denote the absolute value of the mode amplitudes.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The Schwarzschild metric is symmetric under the transformation $t\to-t$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Accordingly, the QNMs for a given $(\ell,m)$ come in pairs with positive and negative oscillation frequencies: $\omega_{\ell m n = -\omega_{\ell m n ^*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The negative-frequency mode is called a mirror mode, since it is a reflection across the imaginary frequency axis of the positive-frequency mode.
elements.sent-2411-078: For the Kerr metric, the symmetry transformation is $t\to -t, \phi \to -\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-079: The QNMs still come in pairs, but the symmetry relation is now $\omega_{\ell m n = -\omega_{\ell -m n ^*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Thus it is the positive-frequency mode for $-m$ that generates the negative-frequency partner for the original mode.
elements.sent-2411-081: Note that because of these relations, tables of QNM frequencies typically only give the positive-frequency values.
elements.sent-2411-082: QNMs can also be described as prograde or retrograde.
elements.sent-2411-083: A prograde mode has $m$ and the real part of $\omega$ with the same sign, so with the dependence $\exp(-i\omega t + im\phi)$ the pattern speed is in the direction of increasing $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-084: The amplitudes of the QNMs depend on how the modes are excited.
elements.sent-2411-085: For typical binary black hole mergers, such as those considered in this paper, the angular momentum in the system ensures that the prograde modes are excited more strongly than the corresponding retrograde modes.
elements.sent-2411-086: In this paper, we will use the notation $(l,m,n)$ to denote a QNM, adhering to the convention that its real part is always positive.
elements.sent-2411-087: For example, $(2,2,0)$ corresponds to the prograde fundamental mode $e^{-i\omega_{220 t}$, while $-(2,-2,0)^*$ represents a retrograde mode $e^{i\omega^*_{2,-2,0 t}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-090: A key feature of the work reported here is that we do not use standard nonlinear least-squares algorithms to fit QNMs to our numerical waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-091: Fitting a sum of damped sinusoids to data is a tricky problem.
elements.sent-2411-092: Note that a single damped exponential can be written in several equivalent forms: \begin{equation}\label{eq:amp_phase} Ae^{-i(\omega t +\phi)} =Ae^{-i\phi}e^{-i\omega t}=Ze^{-i\omega t}, Z = Ae^{-i\phi}\equiv x+iy.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq:amp_phase} Ae^{-i(\omega t +\phi)} =Ae^{-i\phi}e^{-i\omega t}=Ze^{-i\omega t}, Z = Ae^{-i\phi}\equiv x+iy.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: Here $A$ and $\phi$ are real, while $\omega$ can be complex.
elements.sent-2411-094: If $\omega$ is known, then using the first expression in Eq.~\eqref{eq:amp_phase requires nonlinear least squares to fit for the phase $\phi$, whereas the last expression in that equation makes clear that the problem actually requires only linear least squares fitting.}
elements.sent-2411-095: However, fitting for the frequencies, whether real or complex, is inherently a nonlinear least squares problem and generally much more difficult.
elements.sent-2411-096: The next important point is that fitting a sum of exponentials with unknown frequencies or decay constants is numerically ill-conditioned.
elements.sent-2411-097: This fact has been known for a long time (see, e.g., the 1956 book by Lanczos~\cite{lanczos1956 ), and is continually rediscovered by physicists.}
elements.sent-2411-098: In practice, this means that fitting with the wrong number of exponentials or with the correct number but noisy data, can lead to results with large errors.
elements.sent-2411-099: This sensitivity implies that one needs to use as robust a method as possible to do the fitting.
elements.sent-2411-100: The ``best'' algorithm known has also been known for a long time, since 1973~\cite{golub1973 .}
elements.sent-2411-101: It takes advantage of the fact that some of the parameters in the fit enter the model linearly while others are nonlinear.
elements.sent-2411-102: Such problems are called separable least squares, and the algorithm is called Variable Projection, implemented originally in a Fortran code called VARPRO~\cite{golub1972 .}
elements.sent-2411-103: The idea is to start with initial guesses only for the nonlinear parameters.
elements.sent-2411-104: Then standard linear least squares solves for the linear parameters by the usual analytic process.
elements.sent-2411-105: Next, an iterative nonlinear fitting routine updates the nonlinear parameters with the linear parameters held fixed.
elements.sent-2411-106: The whole procedure is then iterated until a suitable tolerance is achieved.
elements.sent-2411-107: The clever part of the algorithm deals with the Jacobian of the cost function with respect to the nonlinear parameters that is used in the nonlinear fitting.
elements.sent-2411-108: This Jacobian has a dependence on the linear parameters because the nonlinear parameters depend implicitly on the linear ones.
elements.sent-2411-109: Ref.~\cite{golub1973 worked out this contribution to the Jacobian---it can be computed explicitly from the analytic solution of the linear least-squares problem using linear algebra techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-110: In general, this algorithm is never worse than brute-force nonlinear least squares fitting, and often succeeds when brute force fails.
elements.sent-2411-111: Although most of the main results we present in Sec.~\ref{sec:results are obtained from simple linear least-squares fits, VARPRO is crucial in arriving at these results.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The ringdowns we analyze are composed of an initially unknown number of overlapping individual quasinormal modes with unknown frequencies and damping times.
elements.sent-2411-113: While VARPRO is the best algorithm we know of to tackle this problem, it is still challenging to simultaneously identify many modes in the presence of numerical noise when the number of modes and their frequencies are unknown.
elements.sent-2411-114: To simplify the problem, we rely on the fact that many modes decay at different rates and that the majority of the modes will have decayed below the noise floor at late times.
elements.sent-2411-115: Given this, we initially employ VARPRO at very late times where only one or two long-lived modes are still present and allow VARPRO to identify modes agnostically, determining the complex frequencies and complex amplitudes of the slowest decaying modes.\footnote{Note that we use varying time intervals to identify stable new modes that show up as we go back in time.}
elements.sent-2411-116: These intervals are longer at later times and shorter at earlier times, when multiple new modes with rapid decay times appear.
elements.sent-2411-117: Once we are confident in the presence and stability of these modes, we move to a slightly earlier time and look for the next slowest decaying modes.
elements.sent-2411-118: To further simplify the fitting, as we step back to an earlier time and agnostically search for additional modes, we no longer allow the frequencies of previously found modes to vary.
elements.sent-2411-119: Instead, we keep them fixed at the analytic values corresponding to the remnant mass and spin.
elements.sent-2411-120: This reduces the complexity of the fit to one or two nonlinear searches at a time, while previously found modes enter into the fit linearly.
elements.sent-2411-121: We repeat this process of adding found modes and nonlinearly searching for new modes until VARPRO can no longer find any stable modes.
elements.sent-2411-122: Finally, in regards to initial guesses, when the number of modes is reasonable and VARPRO is well-behaved, it is insensitive to the initial guess when searching for new modes.
elements.sent-2411-123: Given this, we set the initial guess for the real part randomly from the interval $[0,1)$ and the imaginary part from an interval of $(-1,0]$, which are practical values for the ringdown frequencies of the waveforms considered in this work.
elements.sent-2411-124: While VARPRO is capable of imposing bounds, we set the bounds for the real and imaginary components to be $(-\infty,\infty)$, effectively allowing the search to be unbounded.
elements.sent-2411-125: In this work, we have relied on a modern implementation of VARPRO~\cite{oleary2013 in Matlab.}
elements.sent-2411-126: We have translated this code into Python so that it can use the nonlinear solvers available in Scipy.
elements.sent-2411-127: This Python version is publicly available at \cite{varpro .}

elements.sent-2411-152: Identifying the modes present in the ringdown of an arbitrary numerical waveform comes with a number of challenges.
elements.sent-2411-153: To illustrate these challenges, we first consider an idealized analytic ringdown model.
elements.sent-2411-154: We construct an analytic waveform modeled after the ringdown of SXS:BBH:2423 and its remnant parameters.
elements.sent-2411-155: The analytic model is a superposition of modes, as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:model , including the $(2,2,n)$ modes with $n=\{0...9\}$, the $(3,2,0)$ mode and its first overtone, the $(3,2,1)$ mode.}
elements.sent-2411-156: In addition to these damped sinusoids, we also add a constant term to help stabilize our fits, since such a term seems to be present in our numerical relativity waveforms for reasons that are not clear.
elements.sent-2411-157: The constant we fit tends to be three orders of magnitude smaller than the smallest damped sinusoid, which emphasizes the ill-conditioned nature of the fitting.
elements.sent-2411-158: The frequencies, $\omega_{\ell m n}$, are determined by the remnant of SXS:BBH:2423 and the complex amplitudes, $C_{\ell mn}$, are consistent with those we find below in SXS:BBH:2423.
elements.sent-2411-159: In such a purely analytic model, we can use linear least squares with varying start times to fit for the modes we have included.
elements.sent-2411-160: We recover the expected amplitudes as they decay over time until they eventually reach roundoff.
elements.sent-2411-161: However, in realistic numerical waveforms, numerical noise interferes with the ability to resolve the individual modes below some level.
elements.sent-2411-162: In order to better understand the role of numerical noise, we add a small amount of Gaussian noise to the analytic model.
elements.sent-2411-163: In this particular example, we add Gaussian noise with an average amplitude of $\sim 2\times 10^{-8}$.
elements.sent-2411-164: For context, the numerical noise often present in SXS extrapolated waveforms is closer to $10^{-4}$ -- $10^{-5}$, so the amount of noise added in this example is orders of magnitude smaller than that present in publicly available waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-165: The results of this procedure are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:2423_analytic .}
elements.sent-2411-166: Notice that while the least damped modes are well resolved across a wide range of times, the highest damped modes are most sensitive to the tiny amount of noise we have added.
elements.sent-2411-167: In Fig.~\ref{fig:2423_analytic , the $n=9$ and $n=8$ modes are the first to be affected by the added noise, deviating from their expected decays at around $12$ -- $13M.$ Although the noise is only around $10^{-8}$, these modes become unresolvable at much larger amplitudes, $\sim 10^{-3}$ for $n=9$ and $\sim 10^{-2}$ for $n=8.$ Once the higher damped modes begin to deviate, the next highest damped modes are eventually affected and the deviations cascade through the lower $n$, yielding inaccurate amplitudes for many of the modes.}
elements.sent-2411-168: This high sensitivity to low noise levels even in analytic models means that highly damped modes are likely to be very difficult to resolve in even the best numerical waveforms publicly available, and even when they are really present.
elements.sent-2411-169: The early deviation from the expected decay that occurs for some of the mid-range $n$, say $n=5$, is in part due to the higher modes, $n > 5$, becoming unresolvable yet remaining in the set of modes we use to fit to the data.
elements.sent-2411-170: For example, consider Fig.~\ref{fig:2423_analytic , where we are fitting up to $N=9$, where $N$ denotes the maximum overtone number $n$ used in a fit.}
elements.sent-2411-171: We see how the $n=5$ mode begins to deviate from its expected decay at around $16M$, when its amplitude is still $10^{-2}$.
elements.sent-2411-172: This is well above the amplitude for which we are able to resolve the highest damped mode, $n=9$.
elements.sent-2411-173: One remedy for this issue is to discard the highest modes once they are affected by the noise, which in turns extends our ability to resolve some of the lower modes.
elements.sent-2411-174: In practice, this means that once we reach a time where the $n=9$ mode can no longer be resolved for a given noise level, the set of $(2,2)$ modes that we include in the fit is limited to $N=8$ rather than $N=9$.

elements.sent-2411-189: Having described our methods for identifying modes with VARPRO in Sec.~\ref{sec:dampedsinusoids and our mode-dropping technique in the previous section, we now turn to identifying and fitting the QNMs of two high-accuracy numerical relativity waveforms.}

elements.sent-2411-291: Understanding the nature of binary black hole ringdowns is critical to future endeavors probing Einstein's general theory of relativity.
elements.sent-2411-292: Whether it is black hole spectroscopy or searching for black hole mimickers, it is essential to understand the physical aspects of ringdown signals.
elements.sent-2411-293: Using some of the highest-accuracy ringdown waveforms available from numerical relativity coupled with a robust nonlinear fitting scheme, we reveal details of the ringdown that were previously unachievable.
elements.sent-2411-294: Through advances in numerical relativity waveform extraction, namely CCE, BMS frame fixing, and removing contributions from the memory, the noise floor in the ringdown of these waveforms is orders of magnitude smaller than those previously available.
elements.sent-2411-295: This, along with the use of variable projection (VARPRO), unveils a number of QNMs in the dominant quadrupolar $(2,2)$ component of the ringdown that were previously undiscovered.
elements.sent-2411-296: This includes a number of second-order QNMs and overtones of modes present through spherical-spheroidal mixing.
elements.sent-2411-297: In addition to these newly observed modes, we also improve on our ability to numerically extract the many overtones associated with the $(2,2)$ QNM.
elements.sent-2411-298: Through simplified analytic explorations, we show the high sensitivity of overtones to small amounts of noise and provide an improved method for extending our ability to show stable amplitudes across time.
elements.sent-2411-299: In the ringdown of a highly spinning remnant, SXS:BBH:2423, we find that the overtones have stable amplitudes over at least $10M$ up to the $n=7$ overtone, and the next highest two to a lesser extent.
elements.sent-2411-300: Beyond this, we also find four second-order QNMs, the much sought after nonlinearities that have remained elusive in binary black hole ringdowns.
elements.sent-2411-301: These four QNMs --- $\omega_{440 - \omega_{220 ^{*}$, $\omega_{440 - \omega_{320 ^{*}$, $\omega_{220 + \omega_{200}$, and $\omega_{220 -\omega_{200 ^{*}$ --- turn out to be remarkably stable alongside the many first-order QNMs.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-302: While these second-order modes are generally subdominant in amplitude, the loudest of these, $\omega_{440 - \omega_{220 ^{*}$, is likely the best candidate for future observation and may serve as an excellent probe of the nonlinear nature of binary black hole mergers.}}
elements.sent-2411-303: In the case of a more typical moderately spinning remnant, SXS:BBH:2420, we find an even greater abundance of modes.
elements.sent-2411-304: Because of the short-lived nature of overtones at lower spins, the highest overtones are shown to be stable over a smaller window of time, while the lower $n$ remain more easily resolvable.
elements.sent-2411-305: In addition to finding more modes from spherical-spheroidal mixing, we find up to the second overtone of the $(3,2)$ QNM and four more second-order QNMs.
elements.sent-2411-306: These additional second-order QNMs are subdominant to the four found in SXS:BBH:2420, coming from interactions with the $(2,2,0)$ and lower amplitude $(3,0,0)$ and $(4,0,0)$ QNMs.
elements.sent-2411-307: The $\omega_{440 - \omega_{220 ^{*}$ QNM is again the largest amplitude quadratic mode, indicating that this mode likely dominates the second-order QNMs in aligned-spin binary black hole mergers like those considered in this work.}}
elements.sent-2411-308: Future explorations of more generic systems are necessary to reveal more about the spectrum and excitability of second-order modes in binary ringdowns.
elements.sent-2411-309: Through this work, we show that with the proper fitting techniques and high-accuracy waveforms we can confidently identify and extract the individual QNMs of the ringdown spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-310: We also confirm the importance of overtones and their physical contribution to binary ringdowns.
elements.sent-2411-311: Further, we show there are indeed nonlinearities in binary black hole ringdowns.
elements.sent-2411-312: One kind of nonlinearity is identifiable second-order QNMs.
elements.sent-2411-313: It is worth emphasizing that, while mathematically these count as a nonlinearity, they are computable by applying linear perturbation theory to second order.
elements.sent-2411-314: In addition, there may perhaps be some still unknown contributions in the early ringdown, with relatively small amplitude.
elements.sent-2411-315: The second-order QNMs in binary ringdowns will undoubtedly be targets of future detectors probing general relativity.
elements.sent-2411-316: Further studies are necessary to determine the detectability of these modes in future detectors.

elements.sent-2411-408: Note that there are two distinct fitting procedures used to obtain the following results.
elements.sent-2411-409: The first procedure involves nonlinear fitting using VARPRO to identify the QNMs in a particular waveform, as described above.
elements.sent-2411-410: The second fitting procedure is a linear least squares method coupled with a mode-dropping technique to show the stability of the QNMs identified through VARPRO in the first step.
elements.sent-2411-411: This second procedure is critical to show the stability of QNM amplitudes in the presence of numerical noise as highlighted in the simplified analytic case within Sec.~\ref{sec:fitting .}

Each dashed curve shows the $n=5$ amplitude recovered from a fit including up to $N$ overtones of the $(2,2,n)$ QNMs. This result is consistent with the example of extracting higher $n$ from an analytic ringdown shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:2423_n_decay_analytic} and described in Sec.~\ref{sec:fitting}. This divergence from the trend for $n=5$, with a peculiar turnaround at $\chi \sim 0.9$, is why the $n=5$ mode is often deemed anomalous. The blue dots are the QNMs associated with $\chi=0.76$, the remnant spin in SXS:BBH:2420, and the red dots are those for $\chi=0.92$, the remnant spin in SXS:BBH:2423.
